metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC4's consistency audit scope to include `infra/runbooks/` (specifically `github-mirror-lock.md`), and add an in-scope note to the GitHub-intake child that `github-mirror-lock.md`'s `has_issues=false` step must be removed or conditioned on the Issues-enabled state after the intake funnel ships.
findings.finding: The final consistency audit (AC4) explicitly lists 'CONTRIBUTING.md, CLAUDE.md, SUPPORT.md, README.md, and all new docs' but omits infra/runbooks/ — which contains `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md` with a step that issues `gh api -X PATCH /repos/navapbc/rebar -F has_issues=false` as a 'Mirror-hygiene (OPTIONAL cutover step)'. After the GitHub-intake child re-enables Issues, executing that runbook step would silently re-disable the just-created intake funnel, directly contradicting the plan's primary success criterion. The audit mechanism as written is a proxy check (named docs only) rather than a complete consistency check, and the narrower scope lets this live contradiction survive into production.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (final consistency audit); infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md line 119
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, append 'and `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md`' to the named artifact list, and add a note that the `has_issues=false` line in that runbook must be struck (or marked '⚠️ do NOT run if intake Issues are live') as part of the GitHub-intake child's scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] eb3a must add a link to GOVERNANCE.md (and optionally MAINTAINERS.md) in either CONTRIBUTING.md or README.md, with a corresponding grep AC.
findings.finding: AC5 cross-link completeness is partially uncovered: GOVERNANCE.md and MAINTAINERS.md (created by child eb3a) have no stated reachability path from CONTRIBUTING.md or README.md. No sibling claims to add these links.
findings.location: Child eb3a-91d4-b1c0-4a37; Parent AC5
findings.suggested_fix: In child eb3a, add to the IN scope: 'add a one-line link to GOVERNANCE.md in CONTRIBUTING.md (e.g., a "Project governance" line near the top).' Add a matching AC: `grep -q 'GOVERNANCE.md' CONTRIBUTING.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reframe E1 from 'fork-side UX with `has_pull_requests=false`' to the actual GitHub mechanism (ruleset `update` rule + merge-method removal blocks PR merges; verify whether GitHub still allows PR *opening* and bot-workflow triggering under this posture), matching the existing runbook's description.
findings.finding: The plan's AC names `has_pull_requests=false` as the GitHub repo API field being probed by experiment E1 ('E1 fork-side UX with has_pull_requests=false'), but this field does not exist in the GitHub REST API for repository settings. The repo's own runbook (`infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md`, line 200–202) explicitly states: 'Disable Pull Requests is not a single repo flag; restrict to the mirror posture by disabling Issues, Projects, Wiki, and Actions, and removing merge methods.' E1 cannot be executed as described, and any PR-posture child design that assumes a single boolean toggle for disabling PR submissions is grounded in a non-existent capability.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The four pre-flight experiments (E1 fork-side UX with has_pull_requests=false…)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'E1 fork-side UX with has_pull_requests=false' with 'E1 fork-side UX with merge-method-disabled + ruleset-locked repo: verify whether (a) contributors can still open PRs, (b) the `pull_request` event fires for bot interception, and (c) the PR form is reachable from a fork — the existing runbook documents the actual enforcement mechanism (mirror-lock.md lines 200–206).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a default decision rule for E3-discovered breakage in the AC (e.g., 'fix in-scope if the root-cause change is ≤N lines / touches only the PR-event hook; ticket otherwise') so the executor can act without asking.
findings.finding: The AC line for E3 says breakage 'is fixed (or ticketed via discovered_from) by the PR-posture child' but provides no decision rule for when fixing in-scope vs. ticketing is appropriate. An executor cannot determine which path to take without a stated default or threshold.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — '[ ] The four pre-flight experiments...' bullet, E3 sub-clause
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical default: '…is fixed in-scope if the change is self-contained within the PR-posture child's file scope; otherwise ticketed via discovered_from with a blocking label before the child is closed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add SC entries for E1 and E4 that describe what a passing vs. blocking outcome looks like, analogous to the E2 go/no-go spec already present in the SC.
findings.finding: Experiments E1 (fork-side UX with has_pull_requests=false) and E4 (rebar-ticket trailer policy) are named in the AC with an outcome requirement ('design updated or explicitly confirmed') but have no corresponding Success Criteria entry describing what a passing outcome looks like, unlike E2 which has an explicit go/no-go outcome spec in the SC.
findings.location: Success Criteria §3 (E2 only); Acceptance Criteria §2 (all four experiments)

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a depends_on edge from fc9e to 3b51, or add a note to fc9e's AC that .github/dependabot.yml must not be merged until lockdown.yml with the Dependabot exemption is live.
findings.finding: Dependency gap: child fc9e enables Dependabot (creating .github/dependabot.yml) with no formal ordering constraint requiring child 3b51's lockdown.yml Dependabot exemption to land first. If fc9e lands before 3b51, Dependabot-authored PRs will be auto-closed by the lockdown bot — the advisory-notification model described in fc9e breaks.
findings.location: Child fc9e-677e-a271-4678 (dependabot.yml) and child 3b51-5bff-2d41-44c8 (lockdown.yml Dependabot exemption)
findings.suggested_fix: In child fc9e, add depends_on 3b51 and add an AC line: 'Verify .github/workflows/lockdown.yml with the dependabot[bot] exemption is deployed before activating Dependabot (grep -q dependabot .github/workflows/lockdown.yml as a pre-merge check).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a coordination protocol for the four children writing CONTRIBUTING.md: specify the final section order, freeze the §2c and §2e labels (or switch to heading-anchor links instead of section numbers), and have one child do a final structural review of the combined file.
findings.finding: Implicit shared state: CONTRIBUTING.md is modified by four children (1eb1 top-link, 3b51 shepherded-patch section, ddcd DCO section, 1689 §2c slim-down + pointer) with no cross-child protocol specifying the final section ordering or a coordination handoff. The result is an uncoordinated multi-writer scenario where section sequence, numbering references (§2c, §2e), and the interaction between the §2c slim-down and the new DCO and shepherded-patch sections are left implicit.
findings.location: Children 1eb1, 3b51, ddcd, and 1689 — all write to CONTRIBUTING.md
findings.suggested_fix: Replace numeric section references (§2c, §2e) with Markdown heading-anchor links (e.g., [vote semantics](#vote-semantics)) in all four children, so insertions cannot break cross-references. Alternatively, designate one child (e.g., 1689 as the last to land per the depends_on chain) as the CONTRIBUTING.md structural owner that verifies section integrity after the others land.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pick one canonical term (e.g. 'shepherded-patch fallback') and use it consistently across Context, Success Criteria, and Cross-child protocols.
findings.finding: Terminology drift: the plan uses 'shepherded-patch fallback', 'shepherded contribution', and 'shepherded-patch DCO interplay' interchangeably for the same concept, without establishing a canonical term.
findings.location: Cross-child protocols §4 ('Shepherded-patch DCO interplay'); Success Criteria §3 ('no-Gerrit patch fallback'); Context ('shepherded-patch fallback')
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt 'shepherded-patch fallback' (already used in Context and SC §3) as the canonical term and update the Cross-child protocols header to 'Shepherded-patch DCO interplay' only as a sub-topic label, or simply rename throughout.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a sync/validation mechanism for `docs/review-policy.md` against the code enums (e.g., a CI lint step that extracts `_TAG_SUFFIXES` and verifies the doc table, or a policy that the review-policy child owns an update step in the release checklist whenever vote enums change).
findings.finding: The vote-semantics single-source document (`docs/review-policy.md`) is described as having its table 'transcribed from the code enums' (`_TAG_SUFFIXES` in adapter.py + merge-path tags in voter.py), but the plan specifies no mechanism — CI check, generated output, or periodic review step — to detect or prevent drift between the authoritative policy document and the code as the project evolves past v1.0.0.
findings.location: Context section, Cross-child protocols: 'Vote-semantics single source'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a release-hygiene checklist item (owned by the release-hygiene child): before each release, run a script or manual diff between `_TAG_SUFFIXES` / voter.py merge-path tags and the table in `docs/review-policy.md`, and record the comparison result in the release PR. Alternatively, generate the table section from the code enums as part of the docs build.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an ordering constraint between 71b0 and eb3a on SUPPORT.md (eb3a should land after 71b0, or eb3a's CoC-sync edit should be defined as a targeted single-line patch that is safe to apply atop 71b0's rewrite).
findings.finding: Ordering gap between child 71b0 and child eb3a on SUPPORT.md: both children write to SUPPORT.md with no declared dependency or ordering constraint. If eb3a lands before 71b0, 71b0's routing-section rewrite could overwrite the CoC contact changes eb3a made.
findings.location: Child 71b0-8c03-3961-4863 (SUPPORT.md routing rework) and child eb3a-91d4-b1c0-4a37 (SUPPORT.md CoC contact sync)
findings.suggested_fix: Add depends_on 71b0 to eb3a, or add an explicit note in eb3a that its SUPPORT.md edit is a targeted append/replace of only the contact line, not a section rewrite, and should be applied after 71b0 lands.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an ordering constraint between ed57 and fc9e on docs/releasing.md (e.g., fc9e depends_on ed57 so it appends its sections to an existing file), or explicitly enumerate the final section order in one of the two children.
findings.finding: Implicit shared state: docs/releasing.md is written by both child ed57 and child fc9e with no declared ordering constraint or coordination protocol. Both children add distinct named sections but the final file structure is unspecified, and a concurrent or out-of-order landing could produce duplicate headings, a missing section, or one child's edits overwriting another's.
findings.location: Child ed57-3657-6d0a-4cca (docs/releasing.md changelog step + pin line) and child fc9e-677e-a271-4678 (docs/releasing.md decision records + 1.0-declaration subsection)
findings.suggested_fix: Add depends_on ed57 to fc9e (or vice versa) and note in the later child's What section: 'docs/releasing.md already contains the changelog step from ed57; this child appends the decision-records sections below it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name or link the '2026 Python-tool convergence' reference in the success criterion (e.g., cite the internal doc path, ADR number, or external PEP/packaging spec it refers to) so the criterion is evaluable in-session.
findings.finding: The success criterion 'Release artifacts (CHANGELOG, dependabot, README front door, 1.0 declaration mechanics) match documented 2026 Python-tool convergence' references an unnamed/unlinked target document. An executor cannot evaluate compliance without knowing what the '2026 Python-tool convergence' specification is or where it lives.
findings.location: Success Criteria — final bullet: 'Release artifacts...match documented 2026 Python-tool convergence'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'match documented 2026 Python-tool convergence' with a concrete reference, e.g., 'match the conventions in `docs/releasing.md` §X (Trusted Publishing, PEP 740 attestations, Keep-a-Changelog format)' or link the specific internal convergence decision document.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC line that explicitly verifies the GitHub Issues chooser is rendered with structured forms and can be exercised with no Gerrit account (to match the SC's 'verifiable from the rendered chooser' clause).
findings.finding: The Success Criteria specifies a concrete verification method — 'verifiable from the rendered chooser, not a timing claim' — for GitHub intake, but no AC line captures this check. The AC's coverage ('All nine child stories closed') is too coarse to enforce this specific verification approach.
findings.location: Success Criteria §1; Acceptance Criteria §1 (first bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC bullet: '[ ] GitHub Issues chooser renders at least two structured forms (bug report, feature request); a reviewer with no Gerrit account can open and submit a form end-to-end (verified from the rendered chooser, not a timing claim).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'discovered_from' in the AC or in the cross-child protocols (e.g., specify whether it is a GitHub issue label, a 'blocks' link type, or a custom field) so the AC criterion is unambiguously verifiable.
findings.finding: The term 'discovered_from' appears once in the AC ('ticketed via discovered_from') as if it names a defined ticket relationship or field, but it is not defined anywhere in the plan body, context, or cross-child protocols.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria §2: 'E3-discovered breakage is fixed (or ticketed via discovered_from) by the PR-posture child'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'ticketed via discovered_from' with a plain description such as 'filed as a new GitHub issue with a reference back to the E3 experiment finding' or the specific ticket-relationship mechanism your tracker supports.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit expand-contract sequence for DCO server-side enforcement (e.g., advisory/warn-only mode first, then hard-block after a stated grace period) and a rollback procedure (Gerrit ACL revert steps) in the DCO child story.
findings.finding: DCO server-side enforcement is introduced without an expand-contract sequence or rollback plan; contributors whose current workflow lacks `Signed-off-by` trailers will be hard-blocked with no described advisory phase or back-out mechanism.
findings.location: Context section — 'DCO enforced at the Gerrit server (OpenStack TC resolution 2025-05-20)'; Success Criteria — 'Governance, review policy, DCO, and maintainer info are published'
findings.suggested_fix: In the DCO child, add two phases: Phase 1 — Gerrit `submit-requirement` set to WARN (non-blocking) for N weeks, with the new docs live and the bot commenting on non-signed commits; Phase 2 — flip to BLOCK. Document the Gerrit admin revert command (`gerrit set-project --submit-requirement ...`) as the rollback step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly require that each experiment (E1–E4) whose outcome changes a design assumption produces a named ADR under `docs/adr/` (or an explicit entry in the owning doc, per the cross-child protocol), with the AC updated to verify this before the epic is closed.
findings.finding: Experiment outcomes (E1–E4) are decision-forcing events that can pivot user-facing policy (e.g., E2 failure changes the authorship-preservation promise from `git am` to `Co-authored-by:`), but the plan does not specify that these outcomes are recorded as ADRs under `docs/adr/` — only that 'the policy text says so' — leaving the decision rationale untracked.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The four pre-flight experiments (E1–E4) are executed AND each dependent child's design is updated or explicitly confirmed'; Context — 'Cross-child protocols: Decisions live in the repo … recorded as ADRs under docs/adr/'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'Each experiment whose outcome alters a child's design records the decision in docs/adr/NNNN-<experiment-slug>.md (or inline in the owning doc with an ADR header), and the ticket comment links to that artifact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a dedicated child ticket (or designate an existing first-in-sequence child such as acardiac-clotty-hornbill or nonsecular-arthritic-sidewinder) as the explicit owner of running and recording outcomes for E1–E4 before dependent children are worked.
findings.finding: The four pre-flight experiments (E1–E4) are referenced as blocking multiple children's designs but are not assigned to any child ticket. No child owns executing and recording experiment outcomes; they appear only in the epic's AC. This creates a decomposition gap where experiment work — which has independent value (gates downstream design) and must complete before affected children are worked — has no ticket home and could slip between children.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'The four pre-flight experiments (E1 fork-side UX...; E2 Gerrit Forge Author ACL; E3 PR-event dependency audit; E4 rebar-ticket trailer policy...) are executed AND each dependent child's design is updated...'
findings.suggested_fix: Create a thin 'pre-flight experiments' child (or promote the experiments into the first-sequenced child's scope) with explicit ACs: (a) each experiment executed, (b) outcome recorded as an ADR or comment, (c) each dependent child's design confirmed or updated against the outcome. Gate the three dependent children (PR-posture, onboarding, DCO) on this child's close.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the current `has_pull_requests` state of the GitHub mirror (API-confirmed, alongside the existing `has_issues: false` confirmation) and define an explicit fallback design for the PR-posture child if E1 finds PRs cannot be opened.
findings.finding: The bot-answered-PR redirect pattern (central to the PR-posture child) presupposes that contributors can still open PRs on the GitHub mirror to be intercepted and redirected — yet E1 is framed as testing 'fork-side UX with has_pull_requests=false.' If `has_pull_requests` is false on the mirror, no PR can be opened and the entire redirect mechanism is unavailable. The plan confirms `has_issues: false` via API but does not state the current value of `has_pull_requests`, nor does it provide a fallback design for the PR-posture child if E1 reveals PRs are disabled.
findings.location: Context section ('verified via API 2026-07-09: has_issues: false') and AC bullet 2 ('E1 fork-side UX with has_pull_requests=false')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parallel API check result for `has_pull_requests` to the Context section (e.g., 'has_pull_requests: true/false — verified via API 2026-07-09'). In the PR-posture child's scope, add a go/no-go branch: if E1 shows `has_pull_requests=false`, the no-Gerrit intake path relies solely on GitHub Issues (already planned) with no PR redirect, and that child's artifact scope is updated accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cross-child protocol entry (or an explicit requirement in the PR-posture child's AC) declaring the bot's access class (e.g. GitHub App with minimum-scope installation token), secret-storage method (CI secret / GitHub Actions secret, not hardcoded), and whether webhook-signature validation is required — so the child implementer has a binding security charter.
findings.finding: The PR-posture bot ("bot-answered PRs" component) is a boundary-crossing surface — arbitrary external actors can open PRs or file Issues to trigger it — but neither the epic plan nor its cross-child protocols declare the bot's access class, required credential scope, or secret-lifecycle expectations. The plan's cross-child protocol section governs vote-semantics, ownership-inversion, and shepherded-patch DCO interplay, but is silent on the bot's GitHub token permissions and secret storage.
findings.location: Context section ('bot-answered PRs (LibreOffice/Wikimedia pattern)') and Acceptance Criteria — PR-posture child story
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Cross-child protocols' section add: **Bot credential least-privilege charter (binding on PR-posture child):** the PR-response bot MUST use a GitHub App installation token (or equivalent fine-grained token) scoped to the minimum permissions required (e.g. `issues: write`, `pull-requests: write` only); the token MUST be stored as a GitHub Actions encrypted secret (never in code or IaC plaintext); the child AC MUST include a step verifying the token scope before merge.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the review-policy child, name the specific function(s) in voter.py (e.g. `_merge_coverage_gap_decision`) that produce merge-path tag literals, and specify a validation protocol (e.g. grep for `[LLM-Review:` string literals in voter.py, or extract them into a shared constant imported by both voter.py and the test) so the table in docs/review-policy.md provably covers ALL emitted tags.
findings.finding: The plan directs the review-policy child to transcribe its vote-semantics table from '_TAG_SUFFIXES in adapter.py + the merge-path tags in voter.py', but the merge-path tag ('BLOCK — coverage-gap (merge-review)') is a hardcoded string literal inside _merge_coverage_gap_decision (voter.py:160) — it is NOT a named enum, dict, or exported constant, and it does not appear in _TAG_SUFFIXES. The plan provides no pointer to this specific function or any enumeration protocol, leaving the review-policy child without a complete, discoverable vocabulary source for the voter.py half of the table.
findings.location: Context section, 'Cross-child protocols' — 'Vote-semantics single source' paragraph; voter.py:152-166
findings.suggested_fix: Extract the merge-path tag string into a named constant in voter.py (e.g. `_MERGE_COVERAGE_GAP_TAG = 'BLOCK — coverage-gap (merge-review)'`) so the review-policy child has a discrete, greppable vocabulary source parallel to _TAG_SUFFIXES, and add it to the table-completeness assertion already described as existing for _TAG_SUFFIXES ('Documented vocabulary — asserted by a test', adapter.py:43).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign the final consistency audit (AC4) to a named child or the epic executor, with a concrete AC (e.g., epic comment timestamp + checklist of files reviewed).
findings.finding: AC4 (final consistency audit) has no owning child. The parent requires an epic-level re-read of CONTRIBUTING.md, CLAUDE.md, SUPPORT.md, README.md, and all new docs as one set, with the result recorded as an epic comment — but no child in the roster plans to execute or verify this audit.
findings.location: Parent AC4; no child in the sibling roster
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit task to the last-landing child (or a dedicated micro-story) that re-reads all affected docs as one set and posts the result as a ticket comment, satisfying AC4 mechanically.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
